mn10:1.2: At one time the *Bhagavā* was dwelling among the Kurus, at a Kuru town named Kammāsadhamma.
mn10:10.0: 1.4. Contemplation of the Body: The Section on Attention to the Unattractive
mn10:10.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, upwards from the soles of the feet and downwards from the top of the hair, enclosed by skin, full of many kinds of impurity:
mn10:10.2: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs, nails, teeth, skin, flesh, sinews, bones, bone marrow, kidneys, heart, liver, membranes, spleen, lungs, intestines, mesentery, stomach contents, excrement, bile, phlegm, pus, blood, sweat, fat, tears, grease, saliva, mucus, joint fluid, urine.’
mn10:10.3: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a bag with openings at both ends, full of various kinds of grain—such as hill rice, ordinary rice, mung beans, peas, sesame, and husked rice—and a sharp-eyed man were to untie it and review it: ‘These are hill rice, these are ordinary rice, these are mung beans, these are peas, these are sesame, these are husked rice’—
mn10:10.4: so too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, upwards from the soles of the feet and downwards from the top of the hair, enclosed by skin, full of many kinds of impurity:
mn10:10.5: ‘In this body there are head hairs, body hairs … urine.’
mn10:11.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:11.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:11.3: The section on attention to the unattractive is finished.
mn10:12.0: 1.5. Contemplation of the Body: The Section on Attention to the Elements
mn10:12.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, however it is placed, however it is disposed, in terms of elements:
mn10:12.2: ‘In this body there are the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element.’
mn10:12.3: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a skilled butcher or a butcher’s apprentice, having killed a cow and cut it up into portions, might sit at a crossroads—
mn10:12.4: so too, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* reviews this very body, however it is placed, however it is disposed, in terms of elements:
mn10:12.5: ‘In this body there are the earth element, the water element, the fire element, and the air element.’
mn10:13.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:13.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:13.3: The section on attention to the elements is finished.
mn10:14.0: 1.6. Contemplation of the Body: The Section on the Nine Charnel Grounds
mn10:14.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, suppose a *bhikkhu* were to see a corpse thrown aside in a charnel ground, one day dead, two days dead, or three days dead, bloated, livid, and oozing with decay.
mn10:14.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn10:14.3: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will become like that, and has not gone beyond that.’
mn10:15.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:15.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:16.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, suppose a *bhikkhu* were to see a corpse thrown aside in a charnel ground being eaten by crows, being eaten by kites, being eaten by vultures, being eaten by carrion birds, being eaten by dogs, being eaten by tigers, being eaten by leopards, being eaten by jackals, or being eaten by various kinds of creatures.
mn10:16.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn10:16.3: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will become like that, and has not gone beyond that.’
mn10:17.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:17.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:18-23.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, suppose a *bhikkhu* were to see a corpse thrown aside in a charnel ground, a skeleton with flesh and blood, held together with sinews …
mn10:18-23.2: A skeleton without flesh, smeared with blood, held together with sinews …
mn10:18-23.3: A skeleton without flesh and blood, held together with sinews …
mn10:2.1: “This is the direct path, *bhikkhus*, for the purification of beings, for the overcoming of sorrow and lamentation, for the disappearance of *dukkha* and distress, for the attainment of the true way, for the realization of *nibbāna*—that is, the four *satipaṭṭhānas*.
mn10:24.1: Bones disconnected, scattered in all directions and intermediate directions: here a hand-bone, there a foot-bone, here an ankle-bone, there a shin-bone, here a thigh-bone, there a hip-bone, here a rib-bone, there a spine-bone, here a shoulder-bone, there a neck-bone, here a jaw-bone, there a tooth, here a skull.
mn10:24.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn10:24.3: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will become like that, and has not gone beyond that.’
mn10:25.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:25.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:26-28.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, suppose a *bhikkhu* were to see a corpse thrown aside in a charnel ground, bones white, like the color of shells …
mn10:29.1: Bones piled up, more than a year old …
mn10:3.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed covetousness and distress with regard to the world;
mn10:3.3: he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā*, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed covetousness and distress with regard to the world;
mn10:3.4: he dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*], ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed covetousness and distress with regard to the world;
mn10:3.5: he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas*, ardent, fully aware [*sampajāno*], with *sati* [*satimā*], having removed covetousness and distress with regard to the world.
mn10:30.2: He compares this very body with it:
mn10:30.3: ‘This body too is of such a nature, will become like that, and has not gone beyond that.’
mn10:31.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally, or he dwells contemplating body in body externally, or he dwells contemplating body in body both internally and externally.
mn10:31.2: Or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising-and-vanishing in the body.
mn10:31.3: Or else *sati* is established for him: ‘There is a body.’ Just for the measure of knowledge and the measure of *sati* [*paṭissatimattāya*], he dwells independent, and he does not cling to anything in the world [*upādiyati*].
mn10:31.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:31.5: The section on the nine charnel grounds is finished.
mn10:31.6: The fourteen contemplations of the body are finished.
mn10:32.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā*?
mn10:32.10: When feeling an unworldly neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā*, he understands: ‘I feel an unworldly neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, when feeling a *sukha* *vedanā*, a *bhikkhu* understands: ‘I feel a *sukha* *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.3: When feeling a *dukkha* *vedanā*, he understands: ‘I feel a *dukkha* *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.4: When feeling a neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā*, he understands: ‘I feel a neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.5: When feeling a worldly *sukha* *vedanā*, he understands: ‘I feel a worldly *sukha* *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.6: When feeling an unworldly *sukha* *vedanā*, he understands: ‘I feel an unworldly *sukha* *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.7: When feeling a worldly *dukkha* *vedanā*, he understands: ‘I feel a worldly *dukkha* *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.8: When feeling an unworldly *dukkha* *vedanā*, he understands: ‘I feel an unworldly *dukkha* *vedanā*.’
mn10:32.9: When feeling a worldly neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā*, he understands: ‘I feel a worldly neither-*dukkha*-nor-*sukha* *vedanā*.’
mn10:33.1: Thus he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā* internally, or he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā* externally, or he dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā* both internally and externally.
mn10:33.2: Or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *vedanā*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *vedanā*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising-and-vanishing in *vedanā*.
mn10:33.3: Or else *sati* is established for him: ‘There is *vedanā*.’ Just for the measure of knowledge and the measure of *sati* [*paṭissatimattāya*], he dwells independent, and he does not cling to anything in the world [*upādiyati*].
mn10:33.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *vedanā* in *vedanā*.
mn10:34.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*]?
mn10:34.10: When mind [*citta*] is expanded, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is expanded.’
mn10:34.11: When mind [*citta*] is not expanded, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is not expanded.’
mn10:34.12: When mind [*citta*] is surpassable, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is surpassable.’
mn10:34.13: When mind [*citta*] is unsurpassable, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is unsurpassable.’
mn10:34.14: When mind [*citta*] is endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*], he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is endowed with *samādhi* [*samāhitaṁ*].’
mn10:34.15: When mind [*citta*] is not endowed with *samādhi* [*asamāhitaṁ*], he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is not endowed with *samādhi* [*asamāhitaṁ*].’
mn10:34.16: When mind [*citta*] is liberated, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is liberated.’
mn10:34.17: When mind [*citta*] is not liberated, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is not liberated.’
mn10:34.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, when mind [*citta*] has passion, a *bhikkhu* understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] has passion.’
mn10:34.3: When mind [*citta*] is without passion, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is without passion.’
mn10:34.4: When mind [*citta*] has aversion, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] has aversion.’
mn10:34.5: When mind [*citta*] is without aversion, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is without aversion.’
mn10:34.6: When mind [*citta*] has delusion, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] has delusion.’
mn10:34.7: When mind [*citta*] is without delusion, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is without delusion.’
mn10:34.8: When mind [*citta*] is constricted, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is constricted.’
mn10:34.9: When mind [*citta*] is scattered, he understands: ‘Mind [*citta*] is scattered.’
mn10:35.1: Thus he dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*] internally, or he dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*] externally, or he dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*] both internally and externally.
mn10:35.2: Or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in mind [*citta*], or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in mind [*citta*], or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising-and-vanishing in mind [*citta*].
mn10:35.3: Or else *sati* is established for him: ‘There is mind [*citta*].’ Just for the measure of knowledge and the measure of *sati* [*paṭissatimattāya*], he dwells independent, and he does not cling to anything in the world [*upādiyati*].
mn10:35.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating mind [*citta*] in mind [*citta*].
mn10:36.0.2: 4.1. Contemplation of *Dhammas*: The Section on the Hindrances
mn10:36.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas*?
mn10:36.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the five hindrances.
mn10:36.3: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the five hindrances?
mn10:36.4: Here, *bhikkhus*, when sensual desire is present internally, a *bhikkhu* understands: ‘Sensual desire is present internally in me’; when sensual desire is not present internally, he understands: ‘Sensual desire is not present internally in me.’ And he understands how unarisen sensual desire comes to arise, how arisen sensual desire comes to be abandoned, and how abandoned sensual desire does not arise again in the future.
mn10:36.5: When ill will is present internally, he understands: ‘Ill will is present internally in me’; when ill will is not present internally, he understands: ‘Ill will is not present internally in me.’ And he understands how unarisen ill will comes to arise, how arisen ill will comes to be abandoned, and how abandoned ill will does not arise again in the future.
mn10:36.6: When dullness and drowsiness are present internally, he understands: ‘Dullness and drowsiness are present internally in me’; when dullness and drowsiness are not present internally, he understands: ‘Dullness and drowsiness are not present internally in me.’ And he understands how unarisen dullness and drowsiness come to arise, how arisen dullness and drowsiness come to be abandoned, and how abandoned dullness and drowsiness do not arise again in the future.
mn10:36.7: When restlessness and remorse are present internally, he understands: ‘Restlessness and remorse are present internally in me’; when restlessness and remorse are not present internally, he understands: ‘Restlessness and remorse are not present internally in me.’ And he understands how unarisen restlessness and remorse come to arise, how arisen restlessness and remorse come to be abandoned, and how abandoned restlessness and remorse do not arise again in the future.
mn10:36.8: When doubt is present internally, he understands: ‘Doubt is present internally in me’; when doubt is not present internally, he understands: ‘Doubt is not present internally in me.’ And he understands how unarisen doubt comes to arise, how arisen doubt comes to be abandoned, and how abandoned doubt does not arise again in the future.
mn10:37.1: Thus he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally.
mn10:37.2: Or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising-and-vanishing in *dhammas*.
mn10:37.3: Or else *sati* is established for him: ‘There are *dhammas*.’ Just for the measure of knowledge and the measure of *sati* [*paṭissatimattāya*], he dwells independent, and he does not cling to anything in the world [*upādiyati*].
mn10:37.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the five hindrances.
mn10:37.5: The section on the hindrances is finished.
mn10:38.0: 4.2. Contemplation of *Dhammas*: The Section on the Aggregates
mn10:38.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*].
mn10:38.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*]?
mn10:38.4: ‘Thus is *rūpa*, thus is the arising of *rūpa*, thus is the vanishing of *rūpa*;
mn10:38.5: thus is *vedanā*, thus is the arising of *vedanā*, thus is the vanishing of *vedanā*;
mn10:38.6: thus is *saññā*, thus is the arising of *saññā*, thus is the vanishing of *saññā*;
mn10:38.7: thus are *saṅkhāras*, thus is the arising of *saṅkhāras*, thus is the vanishing of *saṅkhāras*;
mn10:38.8: thus is consciousness [*viññāṇa*], thus is the arising of consciousness [*viññāṇa*], thus is the vanishing of consciousness [*viññāṇa*].’
mn10:39.1: Thus he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally.
mn10:39.2: Or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising-and-vanishing in *dhammas*.
mn10:39.3: Or else *sati* is established for him: ‘There are *dhammas*.’ Just for the measure of knowledge and the measure of *sati* [*paṭissatimattāya*], he dwells independent, and he does not cling to anything in the world [*upādiyati*].
mn10:39.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the five aggregates of clinging/feeding [*upādānakkhandhesu*].
mn10:39.5: The section on the aggregates is finished.
mn10:4.0.2: 1.1. Contemplation of the Body: Breathing In and Out
mn10:4.1: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating body in body?
mn10:4.10: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe out.’
mn10:4.11: He trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe out.’
mn10:4.2: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu*, having gone to the forest, or to the root of a tree, or to an empty dwelling, sits down; having folded his legs crosswise, set his body straight, and established *sati* to the fore.
mn10:4.3: He breathes in with *sati*, he breathes out with *sati*.
mn10:4.4: Breathing in long, he understands: ‘I breathe in long’; breathing out long, he understands: ‘I breathe out long.’
mn10:4.5: Breathing in short, he understands: ‘I breathe in short’; breathing out short, he understands: ‘I breathe out short.’
mn10:4.6: He trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Experiencing the whole body, I will breathe out.’
mn10:4.7: He trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe in’; he trains thus: ‘Calming the bodily *saṅkhāra* [*kāyasaṅkhāraṁ*], I will breathe out.’
mn10:4.8: Just as, *bhikkhus*, a skilled turner or a turner’s apprentice, when making a long turn understands, ‘I make a long turn,’ or when making a short turn understands, ‘I make a short turn’—
mn10:4.9: so too, *bhikkhus*, when breathing in long, a *bhikkhu* understands: ‘I breathe in long’; when breathing out long, he understands: ‘I breathe out long’; when breathing in short, he understands: ‘I breathe in short’; when breathing out short, he understands: ‘I breathe out short.’
mn10:40.0: 4.3. Contemplation of *Dhammas*: The Section on the Dimensions [*āyatana*]
mn10:40.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the six internal and external dimensions [*āyatana*].
mn10:40.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the six internal and external dimensions [*āyatana*]?
mn10:40.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* understands the eye, and he understands forms; and he understands the fetter that arises dependent on both. And he understands how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:40.4: He understands the ear, and he understands sounds; and he understands the fetter that arises dependent on both. And he understands how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:40.5: He understands the nose, and he understands odors; and he understands the fetter that arises dependent on both. And he understands how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:40.6: He understands the tongue, and he understands tastes; and he understands the fetter that arises dependent on both. And he understands how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:40.7: He understands the body, and he understands tangibles; and he understands the fetter that arises dependent on both. And he understands how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:40.8: He understands the mind, and he understands *dhammas*; and he understands the fetter that arises dependent on both. And he understands how an unarisen fetter comes to arise, how an arisen fetter comes to be abandoned, and how an abandoned fetter does not arise again in the future.
mn10:41.1: Thus he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally.
mn10:41.2: Or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising-and-vanishing in *dhammas*.
mn10:41.3: Or else *sati* is established for him: ‘There are *dhammas*.’ Just for the measure of knowledge and the measure of *sati* [*paṭissatimattāya*], he dwells independent and does not cling to anything in the world [*upādiyati*].
mn10:41.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the six internal and external dimensions [*āyatana*].
mn10:41.5: The section on the dimensions [*āyatana*] is finished.
mn10:42.0: 4.4. Contemplation of *Dhammas*: The Section on the *Bojjhaṅgas*
mn10:42.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the seven *bojjhaṅgas*.
mn10:42.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the seven *bojjhaṅgas*?
mn10:42.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, when the *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅga*] is present internally, a *bhikkhu* understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅga*] is present internally in me’; when the *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally in me.’ And he understands how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅga*] comes to arise, and how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of *sati* [*satisambojjhaṅga*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.4: When the *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅga*] is present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅga*] is present internally in me’; when the *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally in me.’ And he understands how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅga*] comes to arise, and how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of investigation of *dhammas* [*dhammavicayasambojjhaṅga*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.5: When the *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅga*] is present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅga*] is present internally in me’; when the *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally in me.’ And he understands how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅga*] comes to arise, and how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of energy [*vīriyasambojjhaṅga*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.6: When the *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅga*] is present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅga*] is present internally in me’; when the *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally in me.’ And he understands how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅga*] comes to arise, and how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of *pīti* [*pītisambojjhaṅga*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.7: When the *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅga*] is present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅga*] is present internally in me’; when the *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally in me.’ And he understands how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅga*] comes to arise, and how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of tranquility [*passaddhisambojjhaṅga*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.8: When the *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅga*] is present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅga*] is present internally in me’; when the *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally in me.’ And he understands how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅga*] comes to arise, and how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of *samādhi* [*samādhisambojjhaṅga*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:42.9: When the *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅga*] is present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅga*] is present internally in me’; when the *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally, he understands: ‘The *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅga*] is not present internally in me.’ And he understands how the unarisen *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅga*] comes to arise, and how the arisen *bojjhaṅga* of equanimity [*upekkhāsambojjhaṅga*] comes to fulfillment through development [*bhāvanāya*].
mn10:43.1: Thus he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally.
mn10:43.2: Or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising-and-vanishing in *dhammas*.
mn10:43.3: Or else *sati* is established for him: ‘There are *dhammas*.’ Just for the measure of knowledge and the measure of *sati* [*paṭissatimattāya*], he dwells independent, and he does not cling to anything in the world [*upādiyati*].
mn10:43.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the seven *bojjhaṅgas*.
mn10:43.5: The section on the *bojjhaṅgas* is finished.
mn10:44.0: 4.5. Contemplation of *Dhammas*: The Section on the Truths
mn10:44.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the four noble truths.
mn10:44.2: And how, *bhikkhus*, does a *bhikkhu* dwell contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the four noble truths?
mn10:44.3: Here, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* understands as it truly is: ‘This is *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the arising of *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the cessation of *dukkha*’; he understands as it truly is: ‘This is the practice leading to the cessation of *dukkha*.’
mn10:45.1: Thus he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* internally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* externally, or he dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* both internally and externally.
mn10:45.2: Or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in *dhammas*, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising-and-vanishing in *dhammas*.
mn10:45.3: Or else *sati* is established for him: ‘There are *dhammas*.’ Just for the measure of knowledge and the measure of *sati* [*paṭissatimattāya*], he dwells independent, and he does not cling to anything in the world [*upādiyati*].
mn10:45.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating *dhammas* in *dhammas* with regard to the four noble truths.
mn10:45.5: The section on the truths is finished.
mn10:46.1: Whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for seven years, for that person one of two fruits could be expected:
mn10:46.11: Whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for seven months, for that person one of two fruits could be expected:
mn10:46.12: final knowledge in this very life, or, if there is a residue remaining, non-returning.
mn10:46.14: Whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for six months …
mn10:46.2: final knowledge in this very life, or, if there is a residue remaining, non-returning.
mn10:46.22: Whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for seven days, for that person one of two fruits could be expected:
mn10:46.23: final knowledge in this very life, or, if there is a residue remaining, non-returning.”
mn10:46.4: Whoever, *bhikkhus*, would develop [*bhāveyya*] these four *satipaṭṭhānas* in this way for six years …
mn10:47.1: ‘This is the direct path, *bhikkhus*, for the purification of beings, for the overcoming of sorrow and lamentation, for the disappearance of *dukkha* and distress, for the attainment of the true way, for the realization of *nibbāna*—that is, the four *satipaṭṭhānas*.’
mn10:47.2: It is in reference to this that this was said.”
mn10:47.4: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in the *Bhagavā*’s words.
mn10:47.6: The first chapter, The Root of All Things, is finished.
mn10:48.2: Terror, without blemish, wish, the cloth;
mn10:48.4: The excellent chapter, unequalled, well completed.
mn10:5.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally, or he dwells contemplating body in body externally, or he dwells contemplating body in body both internally and externally.
mn10:5.2: Or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising-and-vanishing in the body.
mn10:5.3: Or else *sati* is established for him: ‘There is a body.’ Just for the measure of knowledge and the measure of *sati* [*paṭissatimattāya*], he dwells independent, and he does not cling to anything in the world [*upādiyati*].
mn10:5.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:5.5: The section on breathing in and out is finished.
mn10:6.0: 1.2. Contemplation of the Body: The Section on Postures
mn10:6.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, when walking, a *bhikkhu* understands: ‘I am walking’; when standing, he understands: ‘I am standing’; when sitting, he understands: ‘I am sitting’; when lying down, he understands: ‘I am lying down.’
mn10:6.2: However his body is disposed, so he understands it.
mn10:7.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally, or he dwells contemplating body in body externally, or he dwells contemplating body in body both internally and externally.
mn10:7.2: Or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of vanishing in the body, or he dwells contemplating the nature of arising-and-vanishing in the body.
mn10:7.3: Or else *sati* is established for him: ‘There is a body.’ Just for the measure of knowledge and the measure of *sati* [*paṭissatimattāya*], he dwells independent, and he does not cling to anything in the world [*upādiyati*].
mn10:7.4: That too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:7.5: The section on postures is finished.
mn10:8.0: 1.3. Contemplation of the Body: The Section on Full Awareness [*sampajānā*]
mn10:8.1: Again further, *bhikkhus*, when going forward and returning, a *bhikkhu* acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*]; when looking ahead and looking away, he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*]; when bending and stretching, he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*]; when wearing the outer robe and carrying the bowl and robes, he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*]; when eating, drinking, chewing, and tasting, he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*]; when urinating and defecating, he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*]; when walking, standing, sitting, falling asleep, waking up, speaking, and remaining silent, he acts with full awareness [*sampajānakārī*].
mn10:9.1: Thus he dwells contemplating body in body internally …
mn10:9.2: that too is how, *bhikkhus*, a *bhikkhu* dwells contemplating body in body.
mn10:9.3: The section on full awareness [*sampajānā*] is finished.
